title: Fluton
description: Fluton is a confidential execution layer that uses fully homomorphic encryption (FHE) to compute on encrypted data. Users sign encrypted intents that an off-chain solver network fulfils across supported chains.

properties.name: Confidentiality
properties.notes: Confidential ERC-20s wrap public tokens 1:1 into cERC20s, where encrypted handles keep balances and transferred values hidden from public view. Once tokens are shielded, a transfer using them never reveals the amount on-chain, and the ERC-7984 standard underlies these encrypted transfers and shielded balances. FHE execution on Zama and Fhenix coprocessors keeps holdings and per-transfer amounts encrypted, although the initial shielding step exposes the depositor's address and amount.

citations.cited_text: Confidential ERC20s solve this by using encrypted handles to keep the balances and values hidden from public view.
At this point, when they conduct a transaction using their shielded tokens, the transferred value is never shown publicly.
We adopt the ERC7984 (Confidential Fungible Token) standart to allow encrypted token transfers and shielded balances.

properties.name: Anonymity
properties.notes: Confidential ERC-20s under ERC-7984 hide the transferred value through encrypted handles, but transfers move between visible on-chain addresses because users wrap public tokens 1:1 into cERC20s and only the amount is shielded. At the entry point Ethereum is public, so the token holder discloses their wallet address and the shielded amount when entering the system. Sender and receiver addresses stay on-chain even while balances and amounts remain encrypted.

citations.cited_text: Users can receive confidential ERC20s by wrapping (which is also called shielding) their tokens into cERC20s in 1:1 ratio.
This happens when you’re shielding your tokens; since you’re the token holder and you want to shield, you have to reveal your shielded amount and your wallet address.

properties.name: Asset privacy
properties.notes: Each shielded asset is its own dedicated cERC20 contract — cUSDC, cUSDT, cDAI, cUNI, cXFL, and cAAVE each have distinct token addresses on every supported testnet. Encrypted handles hide balances and values, and users wrap tokens 1:1 so the transferred amount stays off public view, but the specific token contract being interacted with is visible on-chain. An observer therefore learns which asset moves even when the quantity is encrypted.

properties.name: Plausible deniability
properties.notes: Entering Fluton requires a visible shielding step that wraps public tokens into cERC20s, a protocol-specific interaction observable on-chain. Ethereum is public, so the token holder must reveal data when entering the private system — at shielding time they disclose their wallet address and the shielded amount. Because receiving confidential ERC-20s means wrapping public tokens 1:1 into cERC20s through an on-chain call, use of the privacy protocol is detectable at entry.

citations.cited_text: Ethereum is public, so you have to reveal some data when entering into the private system. This happens when you’re shielding your tokens
Users can receive confidential ERC20s by wrapping (which is also called shielding) their tokens into cERC20s in 1:1 ratio.

properties.name: Time-to-finality
properties.notes: Fluton is an application-level confidential execution layer with no settlement step of its own, so finality follows whichever host chain settles the intent. Deployments span Ethereum Sepolia, Arbitrum Sepolia, and Base Sepolia, and each intent's irreversibility tracks those chains' block finality.

properties.name: Number of secrets
properties.notes: A single secret is required: the user's Ethereum wallet key, from which the FHE encryption/decryption material for ERC7984 confidential fungible token operations is derived. The same key signs encrypted intents that solvers fulfil, while FHE protects sensitive information such as amounts and strategies, and decrypts the user's own cERC20 balances. No separate spending or viewing key is maintained beyond the wallet signature.

citations.cited_text: We adopt the ERC7984 (Confidential Fungible Token) standart to allow encrypted token transfers and shielded balances.
A network of solvers fulfills that intent, while ERC7984 and encrypted execution with FHE protect sensitive information such as amounts and strategies.

properties.name: Deposit time
properties.notes: Shielding into Fluton is a standard on-chain deposit: the user deposits the asset, it is converted into an encrypted state, and the encrypted balance is transferred to their wallet. No queue, challenge window, or time-lock gates this flow — only host-chain block inclusion applies, which is not a protocol-imposed wait.

citations.cited_text:
User deposits their desired assets

2

Encrypted State

The assets get converted into an encrypted state

3

Encrypted Transfer

The encrypted assets gets transferred into user’s wallet

properties.name: Withdraw time
properties.notes: Unshielding is an ordinary on-chain settlement with no protocol-imposed queue, challenge window, or time-lock: encrypted balances are settled, funds return to a public ERC-20 representation, and only required settlement data is revealed. Latency is bounded by host-chain inclusion where the system runs, and adapter batching affects intent execution rather than imposing a fixed exit delay.

citations.cited_text:
When users choose to exit confidential execution: 

Encrypted balances are settled

Funds return to a public ERC-20 representation

Only required settlement data is revealed

properties.name: Censorship resistance
properties.notes: Encrypted intents route through a network of solvers that fulfils them, and confidentiality adapters submit actions to underlying protocol contracts, with no forced-inclusion path or permissionless fallback documented if a solver or adapter declines to fill. A proof of innocence is also required on every intent, and an invalid proof blocks intent creation outright. Fhenix and Zama also represent censorship vectors.

citations.cited_text: A network of solvers fulfills that intent, while ERC7984 and encrypted execution with FHE protect sensitive information such as amounts and strategies.

properties.name: External network dependence
properties.notes: Confidential execution depends on external FHE coprocessors — specifically Zama and Fhenix — to compute on encrypted data, since fully homomorphic encryption applies logic directly to ciphertext and reveals only the final result to authorized parties. Transfers also depend on an off-chain solver set that fulfils encrypted intents. The protocol relies on these specific named coprocessor networks beyond the host chain to produce and process confidential state.

citations.cited_text: This means data can stay encrypted while logic is applied to it, and only the final result needs to be decrypted by authorized parties.
A network of solvers fulfills that intent, while ERC7984 and encrypted execution with FHE protect sensitive information such as amounts and strategies.

properties.name: Escape hatch
properties.notes: Exiting the shielded state runs through Fluton's own unshielding flow, not an independent host-chain path. Unshielding settles encrypted balances and returns funds to a public ERC-20 representation, depending on the solver, adapter, and FHE coprocessor stack rather than base-chain consensus alone. Privacy is enforced across intent submission, routing, execution, and settlement, so withdrawal cannot proceed without that pipeline.

citations.cited_text:
When users choose to exit confidential execution: 

Encrypted balances are settled

Funds return to a public ERC-20 representation

Only required settlement data is revealed

Privacy is enforced across the full lifecycle of an action: 

intent submission > routing > execution > settlement 
Confidential execution is not mixing.

properties.name: Upgradeability
properties.notes: Fluton's bridge, IntentPool, and adapter contracts are deployed across multiple chains. No public governance documentation names an upgrade authority or timelock. The smart contracts also rely on Zama and Fhenix coprocessors for FHE operations, each sitting behind its own admin controls outside Fluton's repository. Fluton's deployed Bridge contracts are Ownable, with ownership held by a single externally-owned account that also deployed.

citations.cited_text: fhevmBridge: "0xB8DBD4BFd2741E95E3Ae6BE76a4b8014E409e7ca",

properties.name: Client-side proving
properties.notes: Fluton uses no proof system for its transfers — confidentiality comes from FHE, which computes on encrypted data and produces no validity proof, so client vs server-side proving does not apply. A client-side Proof of Innocence is generated per intent, but that is a separate compliance proof of blocklist non-membership, not a transfer-validity proof.

citations.cited_text: In Fluton, a POI is automatically generated every time the user creates an intent.
If the proof is invalid, which means the user doesn’t prove their innocence, the intent is not created.
user inputs are encrypted from the moment an intent is created, execution runs directly on encrypted data, and results are revealed only to authorized parties.

properties.name: Third-party inspectability
properties.notes: An off-chain solver decrypts confidential intent data to fulfil each transfer, so the operator processing an intent sees the plaintext transfer details rather than only ciphertext. The relayer listens for bridge intents on-chain and decrypts the confidential transfer data using the Zama and Fhenix coprocessors before fulfilling the cross-chain transfer. On-chain the cERC20 balances stay encrypted, but the fulfilling party holds decryption capability over user intents.

citations.cited_text: decrypts confidential transfer data using FHE (Fully Homomorphic Encryption), and fulfills cross-chain transfers via LayerZero v2.
Decrypts confidential intent data using Zama (FHEVM) and Fhenix (CoFHE) coprocessors

properties.name: Implementation maturity
properties.notes: Fluton is deployed only on testnets — contracts run on Ethereum Sepolia, Arbitrum Sepolia, and Base Sepolia, with no mainnet deployment. The private testnet is closed and a public testnet is being worked on, placing the protocol at an early pre-public-testnet stage.

properties.name: Post-quantum secure
properties.notes: Fluton's confidentiality rests on fully homomorphic encryption, whose LWE (lattice) basis is considered resistant to quantum attacks. FHE decryption is performed by a threshold network of coprocessor nodes. The per-intent Proof of Innocence is a zero-knowledge proof, and a conventional SNARK over a pairing curve would fall to a quantum adversary.

citations.cited_text: The LWE problem forms the basis of TFHE's security and is considered resistant to quantum attacks.
Proof of Innocence is a privacy-preserving cryptographic proof where someone can prove they are not guilty / not on a blacklist / did not perform a forbidden action without revealing who they are

properties.name: Layer of enforcement
properties.notes: Compliance is enforced at the protocol's intent layer: a proof of innocence is automatically generated every time a user creates an intent, and if the proof is invalid the intent is not created. The check sits in the intent-submission flow itself rather than in the cERC20 asset contracts or the underlying chains, so the application logic gates whether an action proceeds.

citations.cited_text: In Fluton, a POI is automatically generated every time the user creates an intent.
If the proof is invalid, which means the user doesn’t prove their innocence, the intent is not created.

properties.name: Enforcement entities
properties.notes: Fluton itself runs the compliance gate: a proof of innocence is automatically generated on every intent and an invalid proof blocks intent creation, proving the wallet is not on a blacklist without revealing identity. No external analytics vendor or governance mechanism is named as the decision-maker, so curation of the exclusion set and the policy behind it rests with the core team that operates the protocol.

citations.cited_text: In Fluton, a POI is automatically generated every time the user creates an intent.
Proof of Innocence is a privacy-preserving cryptographic proof where someone can prove they are not guilty / not on a blacklist / did not perform a forbidden action without revealing who they are

properties.name: Type of compliance
properties.notes: Fluton auto-generates a proof of innocence on every intent — a privacy-preserving proof that the wallet is not on a blacklist without revealing identity or private data — and an invalid proof blocks intent creation, making the exclusion-set check a hard gate. Separately, selective disclosure reveals only specific payment details to authorized parties for tax, audit, or regulatory purposes without exposing them on-chain, giving users a voluntary viewing path.

citations.cited_text: Proof of Innocence is a privacy-preserving cryptographic proof where someone can prove they are not guilty / not on a blacklist / did not perform a forbidden action without revealing who they are
If the proof is invalid, which means the user doesn’t prove their innocence, the intent is not created.
Fluton supports selective disclosure revealing only specific payment details to authorized parties without making them public onchain.

properties.name: Point of enforcement
properties.notes: The compliance check fires at intent submission, which covers every action in the protocol rather than only shield-in or unshield-out. A proof of innocence is automatically generated every time a user creates an intent, and an invalid proof blocks that intent. Because shielding and unshielding are themselves expressed as intents in the confidential execution layer, the same gate covers deposit, transfer, and withdrawal alike.

citations.cited_text: In Fluton, a POI is automatically generated every time the user creates an intent.
If the proof is invalid, which means the user doesn’t prove their innocence, the intent is not created.

properties.name: Selective disclosure: viewing entity
properties.notes: Holders can view their own confidential balances, since the ERC-7984 confidential fungible token standard underlies encrypted transfers and shielded balances and gives owners self-view. Beyond self-view, selective disclosure reveals only specific payment details to authorized parties for tax, audit, or regulatory verification.

citations.cited_text: We adopt the ERC7984 (Confidential Fungible Token) standart to allow encrypted token transfers and shielded balances.
Fluton supports selective disclosure revealing only specific payment details to authorized parties without making them public onchain.
With Fluton, payroll and contractor payments execute privately, while auditors or accounting systems can access limited disclosures only when required.

properties.name: Selective disclosure: viewing control
properties.notes: Viewing permissions are granted ad hoc by the user when disclosure is needed rather than fixed into a transfer at execution time. Selective disclosure reveals only specific payment details to authorized parties without making them public on-chain, with the user choosing which details and which party at the time of the request. Auditors or accounting systems access limited disclosures only when required, so access is grantable after the fact rather than baked into the original encrypted intent.

citations.cited_text: Fluton supports selective disclosure revealing only specific payment details to authorized parties without making them public onchain.
With Fluton, payroll and contractor payments execute privately, while auditors or accounting systems can access limited disclosures only when required.

properties.name: Cryptographic verifiability
properties.notes: Transaction correctness depends on the FHE coprocessor network rather than a re-checkable cryptographic proof. Each intent's outcome is recorded as an on-chain state transition and funds stay locked within the protocol until execution, but the homomorphic computation that produced the result runs on external coprocessors with no succinct proof an observer could re-verify. The per-intent proof of innocence attests only blocklist non-membership, not the correctness of the computation.

citations.cited_text:
​

Deterministic Settlement 

The outcome of each intent is determined by on-chain state transitions rather than off-chain trust assumptions.
User funds are never held by solvers or third parties. They remain locked within the protocol until execution is verified.

properties.name: Open source
properties.notes: The fluton-relayer backend is published on GitHub and its README references an MIT licence, covering only the relayer and solver component. The protocol's other critical components — the smart contracts, FHE circuits, frontend, and SDK — are not published in accessible repositories under a declared open source licence.

## License

properties.name: Private State Scalability
properties.notes: Confidential state lives in cERC20 balances under the ERC-7984 confidential fungible token standard, where each holder's encrypted balance is stored under their address and updated in place by FHE operations. Users shield public tokens into cERC20s 1:1, and subsequent transfers never reveal the value publicly, rewriting existing encrypted entries rather than appending new on-chain notes. On-chain growth therefore tracks the number of distinct holders, not the number of transactions.

citations.cited_text: Users can receive confidential ERC20s by wrapping (which is also called shielding) their tokens into cERC20s in 1:1 ratio.
We adopt the ERC7984 (Confidential Fungible Token) standart to allow encrypted token transfers and shielded balances.

properties.name: Client-side indexing
properties.notes: Fluton's confidential token is the cERC20, an account-based ERC-7984 confidential fungible token where encrypted handles keep balances and values hidden from public view. Balance discovery is a direct keyed lookup against the user's own account entry, decrypted with their FHE key, so the wallet does not iterate over a commitment set or trial-decrypt outputs.

citations.cited_text: Confidential ERC20s solve this by using encrypted handles to keep the balances and values hidden from public view.
We adopt the ERC7984 (Confidential Fungible Token) standart to allow encrypted token transfers and shielded balances.

properties.name: Private state model
properties.notes: Fluton's confidential token is a cERC20 following the ERC-7984 confidential fungible token standard, where encrypted handles keep balances and values hidden on a per-account basis. Users obtain these tokens by wrapping public tokens 1:1 into cERC20s, and transfers update encrypted balance ciphertexts tied to addresses rather than spending note commitments through nullifiers. Zama and Fhenix coprocessors compute directly on these per-address encrypted balances.

citations.cited_text: Confidential ERC20s solve this by using encrypted handles to keep the balances and values hidden from public view.
Users can receive confidential ERC20s by wrapping (which is also called shielding) their tokens into cERC20s in 1:1 ratio.

properties.name: Private Data Storage
properties.notes: Encrypted cERC20 balances live as ciphertext inside the token contracts themselves, with confidential ERC-20s using encrypted handles to keep balances and values hidden from public view. Intent payloads on the bridge contract follow the same pattern — the bridge contract's confidential path accepts encrypted types that hold no plaintext on-chain but act as handles to encrypted data processed through an FHE system run by Zama and Fhenix coprocessors invoked from the protocol's own contracts.

citations.cited_text: Confidential ERC20s solve this by using encrypted handles to keep the balances and values hidden from public view.
These types do not store plaintext values directly on-chain. Rather, they act as references (handles) to encrypted data that is maintained and processed through a fully homomorphic encryption

properties.name: Access to DeFi
properties.value: Access to external, but limited choice of DeFi protocols
properties.notes: Shielded cERC20 assets reach external DeFi through confidentiality adapters that sit between the user and the protocol, so confidential interaction routes through the adapter contract rather than the protocol's own contracts. The adapter unwraps the confidential tokens to their standard form before submitting batched actions, because existing protocols do not support cERC20s. Each project must build its own adapter and no global one exists, bounding reach to protocols that have one deployed.

citations.cited_text: The Confidentiality Adapter is a smart contract which works as a middleware between the user and the protocol
Since the existing protocols do not support cERC20 tokens, the confidentiality adapter has to unwrap the confidential tokens back to their standart form.
Each projects has to create their own confidentiality adapter contract suitable to their needs. There is no global confidentiality adapter which makes every app confidential.

properties.name: Programmability / Generality
properties.notes: User actions are expressed as encrypted intents describing what a user wants to achieve, which a network of solvers fulfils while FHE and ERC-7984 protect amounts and strategies. Encrypted intents route through a confidentiality router to protocol-specific adapters that mediate private interaction with an underlying protocol layer against existing DeFi contracts, not arbitrary private contracts. Expressiveness is bound by available adapters and the intent schema, not a general private VM.

citations.cited_text: A network of solvers fulfills that intent, while ERC7984 and encrypted execution with FHE protect sensitive information such as amounts and strategies.
which is routed by the Confidentiality Router to the protocol specific Confidentiality Adapter
